message:
fix(cli/self-update): stream release tarball download straight to disk
via asupersync `request_streaming` so `am self-update` works under the
removed HTTP body-size cap (GH#100) and on RAM-constrained hosts

The previous `download_file_sync` buffered the entire response body in
memory and relied on `HttpClient::max_body_size(256 MiB)` to bound it.
That was a workaround for GH#99 but still has two problems:

1. Even legitimate ~40 MB `.tar.xz` release archives must be held in
   RAM in full before we can hash/extract, which is wasteful on small
   hosts (the updater is supposed to be useful from a constrained
   shell).
2. The non-streaming `client.request(...)` path is what enforces
   `max_body_size`; bumping it further just kicks the can. GH#100 is
   the decision to drop that cap entirely from the update path and
   replace it with our own streaming-side accumulator check.

Refactor:

* Introduce `MAX_RELEASE_ARCHIVE_BYTES = 1 GiB` as an explicit
  anti-abuse ceiling (well above the real ~40 MB archive but bounded
  so a malicious mirror can't exhaust disk) and
  `DOWNLOAD_PROGRESS_INTERVAL = 5 MiB` for cadence of progress logs.

* Add a `StreamingSink` enum (`Memory(Vec<u8>)` | `File(std::fs::
  File)`) with a `write_all` method, so the same streaming engine can
  feed either an in-memory buffer (small assets like `SHA256SUMS`) or
  an on-disk file (the release tarball).

* Rewrite the async body as `download_streaming(url, sink,
  show_progress)` which:
    - builds an `HttpClient` with `max_body_size` set to the 1 GiB
      ceiling (cast via `usize::try_from(...).unwrap_or(usize::MAX)`),
    - drives `client.request_streaming(...)` and drains the body frame
      by frame via `Body::frame()` / `Frame::data()`,
    - accumulates a `received: u64` counter, enforces the ceiling,
      writes each chunk through `sink.write_all`, and emits
      "downloaded X/Y bytes (pct%)" logs at
      `DOWNLOAD_PROGRESS_INTERVAL` granularity when `show_progress`.

* Split the two call sites into `download_file_sync(url) -> Vec<u8>`
  (small assets like `SHA256SUMS`) and `download_file_to_path(url,
  dest)` (release tarball). The latter creates the `std::fs::File`,
  wraps it in `StreamingSink::File`, drops the sink so the handle is
  closed before hashing, and `remove_file(dest)` on error so a retry
  starts fresh instead of resuming a corrupt partial.

* Add a streaming `verify_sha256_file(path, expected_hex)` that hashes
  an on-disk archive in 64 KiB chunks via `sha2::Sha256`, so we never
  pull the full tarball back into RAM just to verify it.

* Rework `download_and_verify_release` to create the temp extraction
  dir *before* the download (so the streaming sink has a stable path),
  then download directly to `tmp_dir/<filename>`, log the final
  archive size from `fs::metadata(&archive_path)`, and reuse the
  on-disk path for extraction. The zip-mode branch stays buffered in
  memory because `zip::ZipArchive` requires a `Seek` reader; only the
  `.tar.xz` path benefits from streaming, which is all of Linux and
  macOS.

Net effect: `am self-update` succeeds on release archives larger than
the asupersync default 16 MiB non-streaming cap, peak RSS during the
update stays at the streaming-chunk size plus whatever `xz` extraction
allocates (no longer O(archive size)), and the user sees periodic
progress output for the download instead of one silent line.
